about_text: I am a passionate game developer and use Unity to create interactive games. Take a look at my portfolio and discover what I have made and how these projects came to be.
about_page_p1: I am Youri Mulder, {age} years old, and I am a passionate game developer. I enjoy programming in general, whether that is in C#, C++, or another language, but programming within game development appeals to me the most. This is mainly because games are interactive and the logic you write strongly depends on the type of game you are creating; no system ever feels the same.
about_page_p2: My interest in game development started with GameMaker Studio, where I first came into contact with visual scripting through drag-and-drop code blocks. Later, I moved on to Roblox Studio, where I gained my first experience with real code through tutorials. It did not stop there; I modified code, combined scripts, and experimented to better understand how everything worked together. I carried this curiosity with me when I discovered Unity, which eventually led to the start of my studies in Game Development at the Grafisch Lyceum Utrecht, where I am now in my fourth year.
about_page_p3: After my studies, I want to continue developing myself within game development and keep deepening my knowledge in building strong gameplay and game systems.
fishybuisness_uitleg: FishyBusiness is a casual fishing game. You catch fish, sell them, and buy upgrades. But watch out for the storm that awaits you every day. (year 2)
livebetblackjack_uitleg: Blackjack, but different, a passion project where you play with the dealer and influence the game with powerful card mechanics.
dualdefenders_uitleg: DualDefenders is a tower defense game. In this tower defense, you not only have to defend your own base but also send enemies to the AI opponent. (year 2).
cybrospace_uitleg: CybroSpace is a top-down twin-stick shooter group project created in 7 weeks (year 1).
formulanight_uitleg: FormulaNight is a racing game set at night. I mainly worked on saving lap times and related timing data (year 2).
bugoff_uitleg: BugOff is a shmup created in 3 weeks in a team of 5 (year 1).
worked_on: During this project I worked on.
project_features_tag: These are a few fun features within this project
livebetblackjack_project_info_1: LifeBetBlackJack is a game I started making in my free time. Since I find blackjack a fun game, I developed my own version, inspired by Buckshot Roulette. In this variant, you use power-items (power cards) to influence the game flow. Instead of playing against the dealer, you play together with the dealer: the dealer plays simultaneously with the player.
livebetblackjack_project_info_2: Although the core of the game is there, I want to revisit LifeBetBlackJack in the future. The current gameplay between player and dealer doesn't feel optimal to me. With a rework, I aim to improve the flow, fix bugs, replace the AI voice with visual speech bubbles, and ultimately release the game on Itch.io or possibly Steam, so the concept truly comes to life.
livebetblackjack_cards: For all types of cards, Scriptable Objects were used. This makes it possible to use a single card prefab that is automatically assigned a random card when drawn from the pool. In the project files, all cards are marked with a K, for example K-Jack (card Jack) or K-5 (card 5). Additionally, an example is shown here of how a Scriptable Object for power cards is structured. Each power card contains a sprite to adjust the appearance of the prefab, a type that determines which logic is executed, and a description used for the UI when the player hovers over the card.
livebetblackjack_slotmanager: The SlotManager controls the slot machine during the game. The script handles the rotation animations, displays current game data such as health and rounds, and manages drawing and placing both play and power cards. This makes the slot machine the visual and interactive link between the player and the underlying game logic.
livebetblackjack_selecting: For interacting with cards or buttons within the game, I use Unity’s built-in OnMouseDown and OnMouseEnter methods. In these examples, I use them to select cards and to display a pop-up UI when hovering over power cards. It is simple but effective.
livebetblackjack_powercard: This code contains the logic for power cards within the game. Each function performs a specific action that can affect either the player or the AI, such as creating a new hand, splitting a card, adding an extra ace, or blocking the opponent.
livebetblackjack_gamemanager: The GameManager handles the game states, such as who has won, how many rounds have been played, and how many lives the player and AI have. It also checks the total value of the hands and contains several functions that can affect the player’s hand. This includes, for example, the split function used to split your hand, which is used both by power cards and the normal game rules. All cards are also spawned from the GameManager.
fishybuisness_project_info: Fishy Business is a project made with 2 developers and 4 artists. This project was a kind of game jam with the theme “instantly hooked”. The player is meant to be instantly engaged in the game and not want to stop. We took the “hooked” part literally and turned it into a fish hook, which led to this fishing game. The teachers were so enthusiastic about the game that they said we should submit it for the Dutch Game Awards, and we did.
fishybuisness_fishspawner: With the fish spawners, I wanted to ensure that fish only spawn in areas where the player is (following the boat’s hook). For each spawner, it can be configured which fish can spawn and how many per type. I thought it would be useful to easily see which fish are assigned to which spawners, so I used gizmos to display the fish names in the scene view. The fish stay within their zone by defining their end position within the size of the spawner.
fishybuisness_fishpooler: The fish pooler stores a number of fish so that new ones do not need to be spawned. With two functions (ReturnFish and GetFish), fish can easily be taken from and returned to the pooler. If all fish in the pooler are in use, a new one is created and stored. If too many fish are added to the list through spawning, the fish that are deactivated are also removed. The number of fish that can be stored in the pooler can easily be adjusted in the Inspector.
fishybuisness_fishbrain: The FishBrain handles the tasks that the fish always performs, such as rotating towards the end position or, when the fish is hooked, towards the fishing rod. It also stores variables that all fish scripts need, such as the fish’s end position. I rotate the fish using an IEnumerator loop and a Slerp towards the end position or the rod, depending on the current state of the fish.
fishybuisness_fishroaming: In FishRoaming, a position is assigned for the fish to swim towards. Every few seconds, I compare the fish’s interest value with a random value to determine whether the fish will bite, after which the state changes to FishBiting. The fish is moved using MoveTowards. With Vector3.Distance, I check whether the fish is approaching its target position. Using a small formula (movespeed *= dist + 0.3f), I then make the fish slow down slightly.
fishybuisness_fishbiting: The FishBiting has 3 states to keep track of what the fish does with the hook.
fishybuisness_fishgoingforhook: The GoingForHook sets the fish’s end position to the hook. Only a limited number of fish can move towards the hook at the same time. If there are already too many fish going for the hook, they cannot enter the FishBiting state while in FishRoaming. When the fish reaches the hook position, the state changes to OnHook. If the fish follows the rod for too long without reaching it, it loses interest and swims away.
fishybuisness_fishonhook: This state is used when the fish is hooked and is not struggling. The fish’s position is updated to stay on the hook, and it regains stamina in an IEnumerator loop. When the fish reaches X% stamina, it can choose to start struggling.
fishybuisness_struggel:
When the fish starts struggling, it picks a position within an angle and range of the fishing rod to move towards. The player now holds the right mouse button to reel the fish in.
During this, the fish’s stamina decreases and the tension on the fishing line increases. If the tension reaches 0 (starting at 255), the line breaks. If the player releases the right mouse button, the fish swims away.
I keep track of whether the target position is the struggle end position so the fish does not pick a new one. If the fish’s stamina is depleted, the state changes to 'OnHook'. If the tension becomes too high, the fish is removed from the line and returns to Roaming.
fishybuisness_gamesaving: To save the game, I use JSON files. The game loads and saves all progress when starting and closing the game, and also saves everything every 5 minutes.
dualdefenders_project_info: I made this project with one other developer. For this project, we had to use a pathfinding AI created by a teacher. We chose waypoint pathfinding and used it to create a tower defense game. As a twist on the tower defense genre, we made it playable from two sides, where you also have to place enemies against the AI opponent.
dualdefenders_camera_movement: To switch between your field and the opponent’s field, I created a camera that moves from a start vector to an end vector using a Slerp. Where the camera is located also affects which side of the map you can and cannot hear.
dualdefenders_enemy: For this project, we had to use a pathfinding system provided by a teacher for movement. My teammate and I chose waypoint pathfinding. I created the rest of the enemies myself. The enemies use scriptable objects to easily create different variants.
dualdefenders_enemy_base: In the enemy base, all values are set, and the code that is used when the enemy reaches its endpoint is located.
dualdefenders_enemy_shooting: For the shooting enemy, I use an overlap sphere to check if there is a tower within the enemy’s range. Then, a bullet is spawned and aimed at the nearest tower. Finally, a coroutine is started for the cooldown. The bullet is then moved towards the tower using Vector3 Movement.
dualdefenders_tegenstander_ai: The opponent AI has multiple states and the ability to place and upgrade towers. In the choosing state, it checks what is needed on the map. If there are enough towers on the map, the AI will start building more or upgrading towers, and this is all configurable. To prevent the AI from spamming towers and upgrades, there is also an idle state.
dualdefenders_enemy_plaatsen: The enemies can be placed on spawn tiles. Through the spawn manager, an enemy is spawned on that tile. A tag is taken from the spawn tile that corresponds to a path that the enemy must follow.
cybrospace_project_info: This was a group project for school made together with the artist in 7 weeks. The project did not go very smoothly and there were some setbacks, but in the end we still managed to turn it into something cool. For this project, we had to create a top-down twin-stick game.
cybrospace_camera_movement: Some parts of the map were not clearly visible due to the camera position. I already had a camera that follows the player, and I adjusted the script so the camera can also rotate 180 degrees, allowing you to see more of the map and move around more easily. The camera position is also slightly adjusted so the player is always properly in view, since the player is not exactly centered on the screen. The camera is rotated using a Lerp and a simple timer.
cybrospace_keycard_door: The keycard door only opens when a boolean is set to true. This boolean can be set to true in the inspector so that the door is always open, or so that it must be opened with a keycard. The color of the doors was initially changed to match the color of the keycards, but this is not used in the latest version of the game. The doors use an enum to track whether the door is opening or closing. The keycard doors work with waypoints, where the doors move towards the waypoints, and the swing doors rotate from one rotation to another.
cybrospace_keycards: The doors all have a boolean that indicates whether the player has the corresponding keycards. When a keycard is picked up, the booleans are set to true for all doors that have that keycard assigned in the inspector. On the UI, there is a grid where keycards are spawned when a keycard is collected. The color of the UI keycard is changed to match the color of the collected keycard.
cybrospace_levelselect: Not all levels are always playable, so the level select buttons disable themselves if the player has not reached that level yet. The buttons check the game’s DataCenter to see which level the player has reached. If the button is active, a level can be started using the level tag stored in the button, which can easily be changed to any level. This makes the buttons easy to configure for future levels that are still in development.
cybrospace_wallturret: The WallTurret checks whether the player is within range using an OverlapSphere and an angle (vision cone). A raycast is used to check if there are no walls in between, and then the WallTurret starts shooting at the player. The turret rotates towards the player using RotateTowards. For visual feedback, the turret has a light that indicates its firing range. When the player is in the turret’s line of sight, the light changes color to red.
cybrospace_gamesaving: The game stores everything that needs to be saved in lists within a separate class, which is converted into a save file using a BinaryFormatter. The stats the player ends the game with are carried over to the next level so the player can continue with the same stats. If the player wants to replay a previous level, they can continue with their old stats because everything is saved per level.
cybrospace_camera: The cameras check whether the player is moving around using an OverlapSphere and an angle (vision cone). A raycast is used to check if there are no obstacles in between. The camera’s field of view (FOV) is indicated by a light that turns red when the player enters it. The camera signals all nearby enemies that the player is in the area so they start attacking. The camera can rotate, and this can be adjusted in the inspector.
cybrospace_gamemanager: The GameManager is used to load the death screen and automatically restart the level when the player dies. Once the boss fight is loaded, the GameManager is overwritten and does nothing anymore.
cybrospace_enemies_p1: I wanted enemies that are easy to adjust, so these enemies have quite a few configurable settings. For example, the sight distance, sight angle, and movement behavior can be adjusted. The enemies can follow waypoints. They can also follow the same waypoints in a random order. And the enemies can roam around using random movement.
cybrospace_enemies_p2: I created the enemies’ vision cone using an OverlapSphere, an angle, and a raycast that checks if there are no obstacles in the way. The AI has multiple states to attack, patrol, or chase the player. Depending on whether there is an obstacle or if the player is too far away from the enemy, these states are adjusted.
cybrospace_enemies_p3: The enemies have the ability to alert other enemies within a certain range, as long as there are no obstacles in between. In addition, enemies can be alerted by cameras that detect all enemies in range using an OverlapSphere. When the player is nearby, these enemies become alerted and receive the player’s position.
cybrospace_boss: Time was getting a bit tight, so the boss unfortunately feels a bit rushed. The boss has multiple attacks that are executed randomly, such as a normal attack that fires about one bullet per second, a burst attack, and there was also a plan for grenades, but that was turned into a normal attack due to lack of time. The boss adjusts its position based on whether the player is far away or close. If the boss gets hit too often, it will also look for a different position. The boss uses an OverlapSphere and an angle vision cone.
cybrospace_bossbattlemanager: The BossBattleManager handles the entire boss fight. When the player dies, everything in the boss area is reset to its original state. New enemies are spawned, and the player’s stats are also reset. The BossBattleManager also controls the fade in and fade out of the screen while the boss fight is active. When all enemies and the boss are defeated, the boss fight is completed, the game is automatically saved, and the main menu is loaded.
formulanight_project_info: This project was made for school in year 2, and we had to create a night racer in 4 weeks. The goal of this project was to build an open-world racing game with multiple races available to compete in. The art side didn’t fully make it in time, but the backend systems such as score saving and the Game/Race Managers do work to support the open-world functionality. I built all of those backend systems. I’m proud that I was able to create the open-world system.
formulanight_eindscherm_UI: There is a lot of information on the end screen, so I chose to put all checkpoint and segment times on a separate tab, with each checkpoint in its own column. This keeps everything well organized and easy to read.
formulanight_UI: After each checkpoint, you can see on the left side of the screen how long it took you to complete the lap and where your time stands in the list. This information is visible for 2 seconds after reaching a checkpoint.
formulanight_lapmanager: The LapManager keeps track of the time and number of laps for the current race and then sends everything to the GameSaver, which converts it into a save file. The LapManager is only used when a race has started, and each race has its own LapManager.
formulanight_global_lapmanager: The GlobalLapManager is used to manage things that are always active, such as updating the text for the current lap. The GlobalLapManager ensures that there is enough storage space for all data and loads it when a save file exists. It handles respawning the player and stops the player from driving when needed, such as during the start countdown. If there is only one race available to play, the GlobalLapManager automatically starts that race.
formulanight_checkpoint: The checkpoint saves the player’s position and instructs the LapManager to save times and check if a lap has been completed.
formulanight_datacenter: The DataCenter saves the game in a separate file and loads it again when the game is started and a save file exists. For saving, I use a BinaryFormatter that converts a class with variables into a file.
formulanight_navigatiepijltje: I created a navigation arrow that hangs above the car so the player can see where the next checkpoint is located. Now that the game is a loop, it’s not as necessary, but with the original idea of an open-world game, it’s useful to see where you need to go.
bugoff_project_info: This was my first group project at GLU. The collaboration and development of the game went very well, and everything was completed as planned. For this project, we had to create a shmup with our own theme, and this is what came out of it, a game where you play as a mechanical wasp shooting down other mechanical insects.
bugoff_movement: The player movement is simple Vector3 movement, and I added a dash to it. The dash works by temporarily increasing the player’s speed. To make the player look less static while moving across the screen, I added rotations based on the direction the player is moving, which I implemented using a Lerp.
bugoff_maploop: The map loop is created using waypoints, and all map segments can be placed in an array. The last part of the map is a ghost segment that is removed later. When the game starts, waypoints are placed on the first and last map segment in the array, and the last segment is removed so the parts do not clip into each other. All segments then move towards the second checkpoint, and once it is reached, that map segment is moved back to the first checkpoint.
bugoff_zandhoopje_p1: MR-Zandhoopje was created after we had some extra time. I thought it would be fun to have a creature that attacks the player from the ground with a spike and then temporarily holds them. Later, Zandhoopje was changed to a little cloud.
bugoff_zandhoopje_p2: MR-Zandhoopje is spawned every wave and creates its own waypoint to move towards. The waypoint changes position a few times so MR-Zandhoopje can move towards it using MoveTowards. After passing the waypoint a few times, MR-Zandhoopje starts chasing the player. When MR-Zandhoopje is below the player, it performs an attack. The player can dodge this attack by dashing at the right time. If the player fails to dodge, they get stuck on MR-Zandhoopje’s spike for a few seconds. MR-Zandhoopje then releases the player, moves past one more waypoint, and disappears.
bugoff_powerups: There are several power-ups such as [shield, speed, tripleBullets, HealthUp]. These power-ups are spawned randomly within a defined area. Once a power-up is spawned, it shoots a raycast downward that creates a landing spot on the ground to clearly indicate where the power-up will land. The power-up then slowly falls down.
bugoff_shooting: I implemented the triple bullets by spawning 3 bullets from 3 different empty GameObjects with different rotations.
